papers.title: Stellar binaries incident on supermassive black hole binaries: implications for double tidal disruption events, calcium-rich transients, and hypervelocity stars
papers.author_names: Eric R. Coughlin Siva Darbha Daniel Kasen Eliot Quataert

abstract.text: We analyze the outcome of the interaction between a stellar binary and a supermassive black hole binary (SMBHB) by performing a large number of gravitational scattering experiments. Most of the encounters result in either the ejection of an intact binary or the ejection of two individual stars following the tidal breakup of the binary. However, tidal disruption events (TDEs) and mergers constitute a few percent of the outcomes, and double, temporally distinct TDEs (i.e., separated by at least one orbit of the supermassive black hole binary) occur at the percent level. We also demonstrate that the properties of the ejected binaries are significantly altered through the interaction with the SMBHB, and their large eccentricities increase the merger rate and could lead to gravitational-wave inspirals far from the nucleus of the host galaxy. We discuss our results in the context of observed tidal disruption events, hypervelocity stars, and remote supernovae, such as calcium-rich transients.

brief.tldr: [theoretical astrophysics research paper] A large-scale numerical simulation of stellar binary encounters with supermassive black hole binaries (SMBHBs) reveals that while most interactions result in ejection of intact or broken binaries, a small fraction (∼1%) produce temporally separated tidal disruption events (double TDEs), and ejected binaries exhibit significantly increased eccentricities that enhance merger rates and gravitational-wave inspiral prospects. The study establishes a framework for linking SMBHB dynamics to observable phenomena such as calcium-rich transients and hypervelocity stars via binary disruption and ejection mechanisms.

source_fields.title: Stellar binaries incident on supermassive black hole binaries: implications for double tidal disruption events, calcium-rich transients, and hypervelocity stars

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The majority of stellar binary interactions with supermassive black hole binaries result in ejection of intact or dissolved binaries (≥95%), with ∼1–3% leading to tidal disruptions and ∼0.5–1.5% to double TDEs—most of which occur promptly when the binary pericenter falls within the tidal disruption radii of both stars. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework showing that stellar binary interactions with supermassive black hole binaries can produce temporally separated tidal disruption events (TDEs) and other exotic outcomes—such as stellar mergers or ejections—offering a new observational signature to detect quiescent SMBHBs. Problem setup: This section sets up a problem involving stellar binaries approaching a supermassive black hole binary (SMBHB) in the pinhole regime, focusing on the survival probability of the binary as a function of its semimajor axis ratio $a_{*}/a_{\\bullet}$, while assuming fixed masses and neglecting bound stars and stellar collisions in the disruption rate. Discussion and Conclusions: Scattering events between stellar binaries and supermassive black hole binaries lead to a variety of outcomes, with ejections dominating but delayed double tidal disruptions—occurring with a fraction independent of $a_{*}/a_{\\bullet}$ and spanning from fractions to hundreds of SMBHB orbits—providing a natural explanation for the double-peaked transient ASASSN-15lh.

We discuss our results in the context of observed tidal disruption events, hypervelocity stars, and remote supernovae, such as calcium-rich transients.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/numerical simulation paper analyzing gravitational scattering experiments between stellar binaries and SMBHBs. No observational stellar data presented.
